name: Follow Up on a Delayed Prescription or Refill
description: Call the prescriber's office and/or pharmacy to chase down a refill that's stuck — the e-script that 'was sent' but never arrived, the controlled-substance refill the doctor hasn't authorized, the prior-auth that's been pending for a week.
disclaimer: Not medical advice. Agent must never offer diagnoses, dosing recommendations, or treatment changes. Surface those decisions to the operator via ask_operator.

context.when_to_use: Operator has a refill that has not arrived, is delayed, or is being held up by a process step (prior auth, prescriber sign-off, pharmacy stock, insurance). NOT for starting a new prescription or changing dose — those are clinical decisions and route to the operator's prescriber directly.
preconditions: Operator's name, DOB (read-only), pharmacy name + location.
Exact medication name, strength, dose form (tablet/capsule/liquid/inhaler) as the operator stated it — do NOT abbreviate or guess.
How many doses operator has left (used for urgency assessment, NOT for dose advice).
Whether this is a controlled substance (CII-CV) — those have different refill rules.
Last fill date if known, and what the operator was told about the delay so far.

principles: Refills get stuck in 4 places, and you need to know which one before you can unstick: (a) prescriber hasn't authorized, (b) pharmacy hasn't received or processed, (c) insurance prior auth pending, (d) drug out of stock at that pharmacy. Diagnose first.
Call the pharmacy first — they can tell you EXACTLY where the script is in the queue, including whether the prescriber has responded.
Controlled substances (Adderall, opioids, benzos, etc.) have legal requirements that no amount of pressure changes — a kind, well-prepared call still has to wait for the prescriber to sign in-system.
If you're running out before the prescriber can refill, ask the pharmacy about an 'emergency supply' or 'bridge fill' — many states allow 3-7 days. This is a process question, not a clinical one.
Never accept the pharmacy saying 'the doctor changed your prescription' on this call. Verify with the prescriber directly — substitution errors and miscommunication are common.

phrases.opener_pharmacy: Hi, I'm calling on behalf of [Operator]. We have a refill for [medication, strength, dose form] from Dr. [prescriber] that hasn't come through. Can you check exactly where it is in your system — whether you've received it, whether it's awaiting prescriber approval, or whether there's an insurance hold?
phrases.opener_prescriber: Hi, I'm calling for [Operator], patient of Dr. [Name]. They have a refill request pending for [medication, strength, dose form] that the pharmacy says is awaiting the prescriber's sign-off. Can you check the queue and confirm it's been sent?
phrases.diagnose_stage: So just to be clear on where it's stuck: is this on the PRESCRIBER side, the PHARMACY side, or the INSURANCE side?
phrases.ask_about_bridge_fill: If the prescriber can't sign off today, is there a bridge fill or 3-day emergency supply you can do? Operator is down to [X doses].
phrases.verify_substitution_claim: You mentioned the prescription was changed by the doctor — to what specifically, and when? I'll need to verify with the prescriber before we accept that change.
phrases.pa_status_check: If there's a prior authorization pending, what's the reference number, when was it submitted, and who needs to act next?
phrases.stock_check: If you don't have it in stock, can you tell me which nearby pharmacy in the same chain does? Or should we transfer the script?
phrases.controlled_substance_check: I understand this is a controlled substance and there are limits on what you can do over the phone. What does the prescriber need to do, and what's the soonest that can happen?
phrases.stall_research: One moment — let me check what the operator has on hand.
phrases.verify_readback: So: [medication] from Dr. [name], stuck at [stage], next action is [what], by [who], by [when]. Pickup expected [date]. Did I get that right?
phrases.graceful_close: Thank you. Could you note in the system that we called and that the operator is low? That'll help if anyone else looks at this.

tactics.name: Diagnose the stuck stage first
tactics.when: First substantive turn — usually with the pharmacy.
tactics.script: Use `opener_pharmacy` + `diagnose_stage`. Until you know WHERE the script is stuck, calling the prescriber is wasted effort. Pharmacies have full visibility on queue status; prescribers often don't.

tactics.name: Pause and re-verify any 'doctor changed it' claim
tactics.when: Pharmacy says the prescription was changed by the doctor.
tactics.script: Use `verify_substitution_claim`. Do NOT accept this on the pharmacy's word. Hang up, call the prescriber, confirm what was actually ordered and why. Substitution errors are common and can be harmful. This is a hard boundary — if you cannot reach the prescriber to verify, surface to `ask_operator` before any pickup.

tactics.name: Ask for a bridge fill if running out
tactics.when: Operator is < 3 days of doses remaining and the refill won't resolve in time.
tactics.script: Use `ask_about_bridge_fill`. Many states allow pharmacists to dispense a small emergency supply of non-controlled, ongoing medications. Worth asking — costs the pharmacy nothing.

tactics.name: Track the prior auth if that's the blocker
tactics.script: Use `pa_status_check`. Then call the prescriber's office and use the `referral-prior-authorization` skill to chase the PA. Pharmacy cannot move a PA forward — only the prescriber + insurance can.

tactics.name: Solve out-of-stock with a transfer, not a wait
tactics.script: Use `stock_check`. Operator can have the script transferred to a sister branch or another chain that has stock. Verify in-network status with the operator's insurance before transferring.

tactics.name: Set realistic expectations for controlled substances
tactics.script: Use `controlled_substance_check`. Legal/DEA rules constrain everyone. The right move is to set up the prescriber's sign-off proactively and stop pushing the pharmacy for things they cannot legally do.

tactics.name: Escalate to a pharmacist (not tech) for clinical questions
tactics.when: Pharmacy tech can't answer something substantive.
tactics.script: Politely ask to speak with the pharmacist on duty. Techs handle queue questions; pharmacists handle interactions, substitutions, and bridge-fill decisions.

boundaries: NEVER share or guess the operator's SSN, DOB, or member ID — read back only what the operator explicitly provided.
NEVER accept a 'your doctor changed your prescription' claim from the pharmacy without verifying with the prescriber directly — route to `ask_operator` if verification cannot happen on this call.
NEVER authorise a substitution (brand→generic, one drug→another, dose change, frequency change) on the operator's behalf. Substitutions are clinical decisions.
NEVER discuss dose adjustments, interactions, or whether the operator should keep taking the medication — those are prescriber/pharmacist conversations, not agent conversations.
NEVER agree to controlled-substance early refills, partial fills, or any departure from what the e-script says without explicit operator approval AND prescriber confirmation.
NEVER pressure pharmacy staff to ignore an insurance hold or PA requirement — that's how prescriptions get flagged and rejected entirely.
success_signals: Exact stuck stage identified (prescriber / pharmacy / insurance / stock).
Concrete next step assigned with an owner and timeline.
Bridge fill obtained if operator was running out.
If substitution was claimed: verified or rejected with the prescriber directly.
failure_signals: Pharmacy and prescriber give contradictory accounts of where the script is.
Controlled substance refill cannot be authorized in operator's clinical window.
Prior auth denied and prescriber unwilling to appeal — surface to operator.
Operator is out of medication and no bridge fill available — escalate as clinical urgency to prescriber on-call.

exit_strategy.on_success: Confirm: medication, strength, dose form, prescriber, pharmacy, expected pickup time, any bridge fill, any PA reference number. Operator knows exactly when and where to pick up.
exit_strategy.on_failure: Politely close. Report blocker clearly — separate facts from claims (e.g. 'pharmacy says doctor changed it; this was NOT verified with prescriber'). Present options to operator.
follow_ups: If PA pending, set a callback for 2-3 business days later.
If transferred to a different pharmacy, confirm pickup the same day.
If clinical urgency, escalate to prescriber's on-call line — do not wait for next business day.

required_user_info: Exact medication name, strength, dose form (operator's words)
Doses remaining (for urgency, not dose advice)
Insurance plan + member ID (read-only) if a PA is involved
Whether the medication is a controlled substance
